خمیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خمیر (xamīr), from Arabic خَمِير (ḵamīr).

ex.u: قاتل جِس کی زَد سے خُود محفُوظ رہ سکے ایسا کوئی خنجر نہیں دیکھا بہُت دنوں سے
ex.e: an assassin who can remain safe from his own blows – I've not seen such a dagger in some time now

خنجر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَنْجَر (xanjar). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خنجر (xnjr /⁠xañjar⁠/).

خنزیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خنزیر (xinzīr), from Arabic خِنْزِير (ḵinzīr, “pig, swine”), an old borrowing from Aramaic חֲזִירָא (ḥăzīrā, “pig, swine”). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خنزیر (xnzyr).

خواب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَاب (xwāb), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *swap-, from Proto-Indo-European *swep- (“to dream”). Doublet of سپنا (sapnā).

ex.u: اور یہ سوچتے ہی اس نے اپنی خوابگاہ کا دروازہ آہستہ سے کھولا
ex.e: And while pondering over this, he slowly opened the door to his bedchamber

خواب گاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَابْگَاه (xwābgāh). Equivalent to خْوَاب (xvāb, “dream; sleep”) + گاہ (gāh, “place; hall”). Compare Punjabi ਖ਼ਾਬਗਾਹ (xābgāh) / خْوَاب گاہ (xvāb gāh).

senses.g: an honorific or polite form of address
transgender (a male-to-female transgender person; trans woman)
an eunuch apart of the Moghul court, also employed as a seraglio guard.

خواجہ سرا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَاجَه سَرَا (xwāja sarā).

ex.u: خواہ کچھ بھی ہو جائے
ex.e: No matter what happens (lit. Even if anything happens)

ex.u: خواہ یہ ہو خواہ نا ہو
ex.r: xāh ye ho xāh nā ho
ex.e: Whether it happens or not

خواہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خواه (xʷāh, “willing, desiring”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian خواه (xʷāh, “willing, desiring”).

senses.g: uncalled for, without reason, justification, provocation

خواہ مخواہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خواه مخواه (xʷāh ma-xʷāh, literally “willing (or) unwilling, willy-nilly”). Compare Bengali খামাখা (khamakha).

senses.g: good, well, in a good manner

خوب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خُوب (xūb). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi خوب (xvb /⁠xūb⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian خُوب (xūb). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi خوب (xvb /⁠xūb⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian خُوب (xūb). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi خوب (xvb /⁠xūb⁠/).

خوبصورت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خُوبصُورَت (xūb-sūrat, “beautiful”), compound of خُوب (xūb, “well, decent”) + صُورَت (sūrat, “appearance”). Equivalent of خُوب (xūb) + صُورَت (sūrat).

ex.u: ساتویں دِن جب بادشاہ کا دِل مَے سے مسرُور تھا تو اُس نے ساتوں خواجہ سراؤں یعنی مہُوماؔن اور بِزتا اور خربُو ناہ اور بِگتا اور ابگتا اور زِتار اور کرکس کو جو اخسوؔیرس بادشاہ کے حضُور خِدمت کرتے تھے حُکم دِیا۔
ex.e: On the seventh day, when the heart of the king was merry with wine, he commanded the seven eunuchs who served in the presence of King Ahasuerus, namely Mehuman, Biztha, Harbona, Bigtha, Abagtha, Zethar, and Carcas.

خوجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَاجَه (xwāja). Sense 2 is a semantic loan from Gujarati ખોજો (khojo).

ex.u: میں یہ کام خود کروں گا۔
ex.e: I will do this work myself.

خود غرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خود غرض.

senses.g: alternative form of خود کار (xud kār)
suicidal; of or relating to suicide

ex.u: خودکش حملہ
ex.e: suicide attack

ex.u: خودکشی کرنا گناۂ عظیم ہے
ex.e: To commit suicide is a great sin.

خودکشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خودکشی (xwadkušī). Equivalent to خود (xod, “self”) + کُشی (kuśī, “killing”), or خودکش (xudkuś) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: to commit suicide, to kill oneself

ex.u: شاید اسنے خودکشی کر لی تھی
ex.r: shāyad usne xudkuśī kar lī thī.
ex.e: He may have committed suicide.

خودمختاری.e: From خود (xud, “self”) + مختاری (muxtārī, “power, authority”), both elements from Persian.

senses.g: victuals, meal, food (for humans, animals, or plants etc.)

خوراک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَرَاک (xwarāk). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi خوراک (xvrak)

خورشید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَرْشید (xwaršēd). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خورشید (xvrśyd /⁠xurśīd, xurśaid⁠/).

senses.g: a male given name, Khurshid, from Persian

خورشید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَرْشید (xwaršēd). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خورشید (xvrśyd /⁠xurśīd, xurśaid⁠/).

خوش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خوش (xʷuš, xʷaš).

خوش آمدید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خْوَش آمَدید (xwaš āmadēd), compare with Iranian Persian خوش آمَدید (xoš âmadid).

خوش فہمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خوش فهمی.

خوش گوار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خوشْگُوَار (xōšguwār). By surface analysis, خوش (xoś) + گَوَار (gavār).

senses.g: Khushab (a city in Khushab district, Punjab, Pakistan)

خوشاب.e: From خوش (xuś, “sweet”) + آب (āb, “water”), both elements from Persian.

ex.u: اس کی داڑھی سے خوشبو آ رہی تھی
ex.r: us ke dāṛhī se xuśbū ā rahī thī
ex.e: a pleasant-smell was coming off his beard

ex.u: تیری خوشبو جنت کی ہے
ex.e: your fragrance is that of paradise

خوشبو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خوشبو (xʷašbu). By surface analysis, خوش (xuš, “pleasant; sweet”) + بو (bū, “smell, scent”). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خوشبو (xvśbv).

ex.u: میری محبوب، ہم ایک ساتھ خوشبودار فردوس میں چلیں گے۔
ex.r: merī muhabūb, ham ēk sāth xušbūdār firdaus mẽ calẽ ge
ex.e: my lover, we shall walk together in fragrant paradise

senses.g: alternative form of خوش گَوَار (xoś gavār)

خوف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَوف (xawf), from Arabic خَوْف (ḵawf).

senses.g: an outer covering; case, sheath, cover, shell

ex.u: خول چَڑَھانَا
ex.e: to apply a covering; to cover

خول.e: A hyperforeignism of کھول (khol), which is inherited from Sanskrit खोलि (kholi, “quiver”).

ex.u: family; progeny

خون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خُون (xūn). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خون (xvn /⁠xūn⁠/).

senses.g: eye for an eye, tit for tat

خون کا بدلہ خون.e: Literally "blood in exchange for blood", or "a murder will be avenged".

senses.g: avenue, a broad road, a wide street

خیاط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَیَّاط (xayyāt), from Arabic خَيَّاط (ḵayyāṭ).

ex.u: آپ کا کیا خَیال ہَے؟
ex.e: What do you think?

ex.u: یہ سَب کُچھ تُمہارے خَیالوں میں ہَے!
ex.r: ye sab kuch tumhāre xayālõ mẽ hai!
ex.e: This is all in your imagination!

ex.u: تُم اَپنی بَہَن کا خَیال نَہِیں رَکھْتے۔
ex.r: tum apnī bahan kā xayāl nahī̃ rakhte.
ex.e: You don't take care of your sister.

خیال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَیال, borrowed from Arabic خَيال (ḵayāl).

senses.g: Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (a province in northwestern Pakistan)

خیبر پختونخوا.e: From خیبر (xaibar, “Khyber”) + پختونخوا (paxtūnxvā, “Land of the Pashtun”), literally “the Khyber side of the Pashtun heartland”. Compare Pashto خېبر پښتونخوا (xēbar paẍtunxwâ).

ex.u: خیر، میں بہت اپنے بارے بات کر رہی ہوں، تمہارے وہاں کیا چل رہا ہے؟
ex.r: xair, ma͠i bahot apne bāre bāt kar rahī hū̃, tumhāre vahā̃ kyā cal rahā hai?
ex.e: Anyway, I'm talking a lot about myself, what's going on with you?

خیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَیْر (xayr), borrowed from Arabic خَيْر (ḵayr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian خَیْر (xayr), borrowed from Arabic خَيْر (ḵayr).

senses.g: Khairpur (a city in Khairpur district, Sindh, Pakistan)

ex.u: سب خیریت ہے؟
ex.e: Is everything fine?

خیریت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَیْرِیَّت (xayriyyat), from Arabic خَيْرِيَّة (ḵayriyya).

خیلے.e: From Classical Persian خیلی (xaylē, “many; very”), from Arabic خَيْل (ḵayl, “horses”).

خیمہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic خَيْمَة (ḵayma) via Classical Persian خَیمَه (xayma).

senses.g: The eleventh letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by خ and followed by ڈ.

داخل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَاخِل (dāxil), borrowed from Arabic دَاخِل (dāḵil).

senses.g: internal, domestic, of or pertaining to the interior

داخلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَاخِلِی (dāxilī), from Arabic دَاخِلِيّ (dāḵiliyy). First attested in 1799 as داخلی (daxly /⁠dāxilī⁠/).

senses.g: respectful address for an older / elderly man
a man who has raised or fostered a child like his own
used to refer to a respected elder of an elder

دادا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دادا (dada /⁠dādā⁠/) (c. 1564), from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀤𑀸𑀤𑁆𑀤 (*dādda) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), an onomatopoeic nursery reduplication.

senses.g: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu (a union territory of India; capital: Daman)

دادرا و نگر حویلی و دمن و دیو.c: دادرا و نَگَر حَوَیْلی و دَمَن و دِیو
دادرا و نگر حویلی و دمن و دیو.h: दादरा ओ नगर हवय्ली ओ दमन ओ दियो
دادرا و نگر حویلی و دمن و دیو.ipa: /d̪ɑːd̪.ɾɑː oː nə.ɡəɾ.ɦə.ʋeː.liː oː d̪ə.mən oː d̪iː.oː/
دادرا و نگر حویلی و دمن و دیو.e: Calque of English Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu. Compare Hindi दादरा और नगर हवेली एवं दमन और दीव (dādrā aur nagar havelī evã daman aur dīv)

senses.g: Dadu (a city in Dadu district, Sindh, Pakistan)

دادو.e: Named after Syed Dad Muhammad Shah Alias Dadu, the founder of the city.

دادی.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀤𑀸𑀤𑁆𑀤 (*dādda). + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-) First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi دادی (dādī)ِ.

داردر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit दारिद्र (dāridra). Doublet of دارِد (dārid).

دارو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَارُو (dārū, “medicine, drug, gunpowder”), itself derived from Middle Persian dʾlwkˈ (dārūg, “drug, medicament”).
From Sanskrit दारु (dāru), from Sanskrit root दृ (dṛ). Cognate with Ancient Greek δρῦς (drûs), Old Persian 𐎭𐎠𐎽𐎢𐎺 (d-a-ru-u-v /dāruv/), Old English trēow (whence English tree).

senses.g: Darel (a city in Darel district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)

داڑھی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑀸𑀠𑀺𑀆 (dāḍhiā), from Sanskrit दाढिका (dāḍhikā, “beard”). Cognate with Sindhi ڏاڙِهي (ḏāṛhī), Punjabi ਦਾੜ੍ਹੀ (dāṛhī), Saraiki ݙاڑھی (ḏāṛhī), Gujarati દાઢી (dāḍhī).

داستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَاسْتَان (dāstān). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi داستاں (dastaṉ).

senses.g: Dagestan (a republic and federal subject of Russia, located in the Caucasus region)

دال.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀸𑀮𑀻 (dālī), from Sanskrit दाल (dāla), itself from दलति (dalati, “he splits”); see दॄ (dṝ, “to tear, divide, split”). Cognate with Marathi डाळ (ḍāḷ).

senses.g: Dalbandin (a city in Chagai district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ex.u: دوردرشن انچے یا سستے داموں پر خریدو
ex.r: dūrdarśan ū̃ce yā saste dāmõ par xarīdẽ
ex.e: Buy TV at a high or cheap prices

دام.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀫𑁆𑀫 (damma, “coin”), from Sanskrit द्रम्म (dramma), from Ancient Greek δραχμή (drakhmḗ).
Inherited from Prakrit दाम (dāma), from Sanskrit दाम (dāma, “rope”).

داماد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَامَاد (dāmād). Doublet of جَن٘وَائِی (jãvāī). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi داماد (damad /⁠dāmād⁠/).

senses.g: hem of a garment; skirt, petticoat

دانت.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دانت (dā̃t /⁠dānt⁠/) / داند (dā̃d /⁠dānd⁠/) / دند (/⁠dand⁠/) / دنت (/⁠dant⁠/), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀁𑀢 (daṃta), from Sanskrit दन्त (dánta), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hdántas, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₃dónt-o-s, thematized from *h₃dónts (“tooth”). Doublet of دَنْدَان (dandān).

دانتی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀬𑀸 (dattiyā) (with Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-)), from Sanskrit दात्र (dā́tra).

senses.g: a male given name, Danish, from Persian

دانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَانَه (dāna), from Proto-Indo-European *dʰoHnéh₂. Cognate with Sanskrit धान्य (dhānya).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

داؤد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian داوُد (dāwud), from Arabic دَاوُد (dāwud).

داہنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀸𑀳𑀺𑀡 (dāhiṇa), from a derivative of Sanskrit दक्षिण (dákṣiṇa), from Proto-Indo-European *deḱs-. Cognates include Early Assamese ডাহিন (ḍahino), Ancient Greek δεξιός (dexiós), and Latin dexter.

دایاں.e: From Sanskrit दक्षिण (dákṣiṇa). From Proto-Indo-European *deḱs-. Cognates include Ancient Greek δεξιός (dexiós) and Latin dexter.

دائرہ.e: Borrowed from Persian دایره, borrowed from Arabic دَائِرَة (dāʔira).

دائرۃ المعارف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دائِرَةُالْمَعارِف (dā'irat-ul-ma'ārif), from Arabic دَائِرَةُ مَعَارِف (dāʔiratu maʕārif).

دباؤ.e: Related to دبانا (dabānā, “to press”). Cognate Hindi दबाव (dabāv).

senses.g: inflection of دبانا (dabānā): — second-person singular informal imperative present
inflection of دبانا (dabānā): — second-person plural informal present imperative
inflection of دبانا (dabānā): — second-person plural subjunctive

دباؤ.e: Related to دبانا (dabānā, “to press”). Cognate Hindi दबाव (dabāv).

senses.g: to be restrained, be kept back
to be suppressed, be repressed, be quelled

دبنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀩𑁆𑀩𑀇 (dabbaï), from Dravidian.

senses.g: Dubai (an emirate of the United Arab Emirates)
Dubai (the capital city of Dubai emirate, United Arab Emirates)

ex.u:
قطرہ میں دجلہ دکھائی نہ دے اور جزو میں کل
کھیل لڑکوں کا ہوا دیدۂ بینا نہ ہوا
qatre mẽ dajlā dikhāī na de aur juzva mẽ kul
khel laṛkõ kā huā(,) dīdā-e-bīnā na huā
ex.e:
in but a drop the Tigris cannot be seen and (yet) in a part, all (can be seen)
it was a children's game, not a seeing-eye

دجلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دجله (dajla, dijla), from Arabic دِجْلَة (dijla).

دختر.e: PIE word *dʰugh₂tḗr Borrowed from Classical Persian دُخْتَر (duxtar, “daughter”).

senses.g: a vault or tomb for the dead; a coffin

در.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian در (dar). Doublet of دوار (dvār).

ex.u: ہمارے بیچ دراڑ پڑ گئی ہے۔
ex.r: hamāre bic darār paṛ gaī hai.
ex.e: We have had a falling out.

دراڑ.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *दराकार (*darākāra), from दर (dara, “cleaving, split”) + आकार (ākāra, “shape, form”). Cognate to Punjabi دراڑ (drāṛ).

senses.g: in reality, in fact, actually, truly, in essence, in origin

دراصل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دراصل (dar asl, “essentially, substantially, actually”), from در (dar, “at, in”) + اصل (asl). By surface analysis, دَر (dar) + اَصَل (asal). Compare Punjabi دَراَصَل (daraṣal).

senses.g: royal audience (prominent citizen's gathering to meet bureaucrats), court gathering
audience hall, court of a noble
degree (a unit of measurement of temperature on any of several scales, such as Celsius)

درجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرَجَه (daraja, “degree, pitch, level”), from Arabic دَرَجَة (daraja).

درخش.e: From Classical Persian درخش (diraxš, “shine; lustre; glister”).

درد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian درد (dard), from Middle Persian dlt' (dard, “pain”).

درد ناک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دردناک (dardnāk), literally دَرد (dard, “pain”) + ـناک (-nāk, “-ful”).

درس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرْس (dars), from Arabic دَرْس (dars).

درس گاہ.e: From درس (dars, “lesson, course of studies”) + ـگاه (-gāh, “place suffix”).

senses.g: in good condition; unbroken, in working order

درست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian درست (durust), from Middle Persian drw(y)st' (drust), from Old Persian [script needed] (*dᵘruvišta), ultimately from Proto-Iranian *druwištah.

درگاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian درگاه (dargāh). Compare Gujarati દરગાહ (dargāh).

درگت.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit दुर्गति (durgati, “ill condition, difficult path”).

ex.u: دونوں کے دَرْمِیان
ex.e: between the two

درمیان.e: Univerbation of Classical Persian در میان (dar miyān).
Univerbation of Classical Persian در میان (dar miyān).

درمیانی.e: درمیان (darmiyān, “in the middle or midst”) + ـی (-ī).

دروازہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرْوَازَه (darwāza). Doublet of دَر (dar) and دْوَار (dvār).

senses.g: Salawat (durood – the uttering of specific phrases to bless the prophet Muhammad and his family whenever he is mentioned or encountered in a text)

درود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian درود (/⁠durūd⁠/), from Middle Persian drwd (drōd). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi درود (drvd /⁠drūd⁠/),

درویش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian درویش (darwēš), from Middle Persian dlgwš (driyōš). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi درویش (drvyś /⁠darveś⁠/).

senses.g: Darvesh (one of the 313 darvesh of Qadian)

درویش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian درویش (darwēš), from Middle Persian dlgwš (driyōš). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi درویش (drvyś /⁠darveś⁠/).

senses.g: Darhal (a town, a tehsil in Rajouri district, Jammu and Kashmir, India)

دری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرِی (darī), from دَر (dar, “court”) + ـِی (-ī). By surface analysis, دَر (dar) + ـِی (-ī).

ex.u: وہ دریا کنارے کھڑی ہے
ex.e: she is standing at the bank of the river

دریا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرْیَا (daryā), from Middle Persian [script needed] (dlydʾp̄ /⁠drayāb⁠/, “sea”), from Old Persian 𐎭𐎼𐎹 (d-r-y /⁠draya⁠/, “ocean, sea”) + Middle Persian ʾp̄ (āb).

دریاچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرْیاچَه (daryāča), diminutive of دریا (daryā, “sea”).

دریائی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرْیَائِی (daryā'ī), from دَرْیَا (daryā) + ـِی (-ī).

senses.g: Dureji (a city in Lasbela district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

دریودھن.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit दुर्योधन (duryodhana, “difficult to fight with”).

دزد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دُزْد (duzd), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *duš- (“bad, wrong”).

دس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀲 (dasa), from Sanskrit दश (dáśa).

ex.u: دَسْت خَطی مُہِم
ex.e: signature campaign

دستار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَسْتَار (dastār), from دَسْت (dast) + ـَار (-ār). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi دستار (dstar /⁠dastār⁠/).

senses.g: bracelet, or an ornament worn on hands

دستانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دستانه (dastāna). First attested as Middle Hindi دستانہ (dastāna).

senses.g: bond, deed, a note of hand

دستخط.e: From Classical Persian دَسْتْخَطّ (dastxatt). Borrowed from Classical Persian دستخط (dastxat)

senses.g: an army formation, typically a troop or company

دستہ.e: From Classical Persian دسته (dasta, “bunch, bundle; group, cohort; handle, hilt”).

دسمبر.e: Borrowed from Portuguese dezembro, influenced by English December, ultimately from Latin December. Compare Konkani देझेंब्र (dejhembra), Marathi दिसेंबर (disembar).

دسواں.e: Inherited from Old Hindi दसवै (dasavai), दसवैं (dasava͠i, Rājasthānī manuscripts) (with adverbializing affix -ai), from Prakrit 𑀤𑀲𑀫 (dasama), from Sanskrit दशम (daśamá). Doublet of دَہُم (dahum, dahm). By surface analysis, دَس (das) + ـوَاں (vā̃).

ex.u:
کُچھ بات ہَے کِہ ہَسْتی مِٹْتی نَہِیں ہَماری
صَدِیوں رَہَا ہَے دُشْمَن دَورِ زَماں ہَمارا
kuch bāt hai ki hastī miṭtī nahī̃ hamārī
ex.r: sadiyõ rahā hai duśman daur-i zamā̃ hamārā
ex.e:
There is something about our existence for it doesn't get wiped
Even though, for centuries, the time-cycle has been our enemy.

دشمن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دشمن (dušman). Compare Punjabi دُشْمَݨ (duśmaṇ).

دشمنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دُشْمَنِی (dušmanī), from دُشْمَن (dušman) + ـِی (-ī).

ex.u: آپ کی دُعا
ex.e: It's your prayer (it's thanks to you); I'm fine, thanks

senses.g: a formulaic phrase of good hope used in letters

دعا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دُعَا (du'ā). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi دعا (d'a /⁠du'ā⁠/).

senses.g: a female given name, Dua, from Arabic

دعا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دُعَا (du'ā). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi دعا (d'a /⁠du'ā⁠/).

دعائے ربانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دعای ربانی (du'ā-yi rabbānī).

senses.g: invite, request (to join a movement, or purpose)

دعوت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَعْوَت (da'wat), borrowed from Arabic دَعْوَة (daʕwa), from دَعَا (daʕā).

دعوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَعْویٰ (da'wā), from Arabic دَعْوَى (daʕwā).

دفاع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِفَاع (difā'), from Arabic دِفَاع (difāʕ).

senses.g: a clause or section of a document

دفعہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَفْعَه (daf'a), from Arabic دَفْعَة (dafʕa).

دفن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دفن (dafn), from Arabic دَفْن (dafn).

دفنانا.e: From دَفْن (dafn) + ـَانا (-ānā) by analogy with Classical Persian دَفْن کَرْدَن (dafn kardan). Compare Gujarati દફનાવવું (daphnāvvũ).

دقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِقَّت (diqqat, “accuracy; attention”), itself from Arabic دِقَّة (diqqa).

دقیقہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic دَقِيقَة (daqīqa) (through Classical Persian دَقِیقَه (daqīqa)).

دکان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دُکَان (dukān), borrowed from Arabic دُكَّان (dukkān), borrowed from Aramaic דּוּכָּנָא.

دکشن.e: From Sanskrit दक्षिण (dakṣiṇa), from Proto-Indo-European *deḱs-. Cognate with Lithuanian dešinė, Ancient Greek δεξιός (dexiós), Latin dexter.
From Sanskrit दक्षिण (dakṣiṇa), from Proto-Indo-European *deḱs-. Cognate with Lithuanian dešinė, Ancient Greek δεξιός (dexiós), Latin dexter.

دکھانا.e: Urdu verb set دُکْھنا (dukhnā) دُکھانا (dukhānā) Causative form of دُکھنا (dukhnā, “to ache; pain”)

دکھاوا.e: From دِکھانا (dikhānā, “to show; to show off”) + ـاوا (-oā, nominal suffix).

دکھنا.e: Urdu verb set دِکْھنا (dikhnā) دیکْھنا (dekhnā) دِکھانا (dikhānā) دِکھوانا (dikhvānā)Intransitive form of دیکھنا .

Urdu verb set دُکْھنا (dukhnā) دُکھانا (dukhānā) Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀔𑁂𑀇 (dukkhei), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀔𑀻𑀬𑀢𑀺 (dukhīyati), from Sanskrit दुःखति (duḥkhati), denominal verb from दुः॒ख (duḥkhá), from दुस्- (dus-) + possibly ख (khá), influenced by सु॒ख (sukhá).

دل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِل (dil). First attested in c. 1265 as Old Hindi دل (dl) and later as Middle Hindi دل (dl).

دلچسپ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِلْچَسْپ (dilčasp). By surface analysis, دِل (dil, “heart”) + چَسْپ (casp, “enjoined”).

دلدادہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دلداده (dildāda, literally “one who has given his heart”).

senses.g: attractive, interesting, engaging, pleasant, lovely, charming, winsome

دلکش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِلْکَش (dilkaš, “charming”). By surface analysis, دِل (dil) + کَش (kaś)
